topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun slanted across my patio as I was watering my petunias when the voice cut through the quiet. "Excuse me? I was wondering if I could possibly use a bit of your yard for a garden?" I straightened up slowly, the hose dripping rhythmically from my hand. My heart gave a sudden, tight thump against my ribs. I turned toward the sound, and there he was—Mr. Henderson, a man whose smile always seemed a little too wide, standing at the edge of my property line, his eyes fixed intently on the overgrown patch beside my fence. I swallowed, my mouth suddenly dry. I didn't offer an immediate reply, just stared at the tangled rose bushes, trying to decide if I should pretend I hadn't heard him at all.

[story 2]

I was meticulously pruning the lavender, the sharp snip of the shears the only sound breaking the stillness of the evening. Then, a knock—three firm raps on my front door. It was him. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the deadbolt. When I opened the door, his presence seemed to fill the hallway, heavy and unexpected. "Hello," he began, his voice smooth as polished stone. "I had a small request. Could I borrow a section of your lawn for some vegetables?" A chill traced a path down my spine. I could feel my palms growing clammy. I took a deliberate step back, putting a small console table between us, my gaze darting toward the darkened window overlooking the street.

[story 3]

The request arrived via a note slipped under my door—a thick piece of cardstock bearing a looping, overly cheerful script. It was about a garden plot, a small corner of my otherwise perfectly maintained lawn. I reread the words twice. My breath hitched in my throat. When I finally stepped outside to confront him, the air felt thick, almost viscous. He was standing near the boundary, looking less like a neighbor and more like an observer cataloging my property. "I was hoping," he continued, his tone unusually earnest, "to utilize some space here for cultivation." I kept my posture rigid, my jaw tight. I could feel the need to retreat, to find cover, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot, unable to move away from the strange tension emanating from him.
